title: Implement FaviconLoader
description: Create AggregatorApp/Common/FaviconLoader.swift as a Swift actor (or serial-queue class) with a shared singleton. Exposes func icon(forFeedURL: String) async -> UIImage?. Internally: (1) derives host from feedURL via URLComponents; (2) builds icon URL https://icons.duckduckgo.com/ip3/<host>.ico; (3) lookup order: in-memory NSCache<NSString,UIImage> → on-disk Caches/favicons/<sanitized-host>.png → network via URLSession.shared; (4) on network success writes to disk then memory; (5) swallows all failures, returning nil. Also expose a static func iconURL(forFeedURL:) -> URL? as a pure testable helper for the URL-derivation logic.
acceptance_criteria: FaviconLoader.shared.icon(forFeedURL:) returns UIImage on success and nil on any failure without throwing
iconURL(forFeedURL:) returns the correct DuckDuckGo URL for a valid feed URL and nil for an invalid URL
Concurrent calls for the same host deduplicate (actor isolation or serial queue)
No CF/Cloudflare auth headers injected — plain URLSession.shared

handoff_summary.completed: FaviconLoader.swift created and confirmed compiling. Implements: (1) actor isolation for thread safety, (2) in-flight Task deduplication so parallel callers for the same host share one network round-trip, (3) NSCache memory layer, (4) on-disk Caches/favicons/<host>.png layer with sanitized filenames, (5) URLSession.shared network fetch with no CF headers, (6) all failures silently return nil, (7) static iconURL(forFeedURL:) pure helper for testability.
handoff_summary.risks: The UIKit SourceKit diagnostic ("No such module 'UIKit'") is a false positive from the non-iOS LSP environment — the xcodebuild compile confirms it is not a real issue.
handoff_summary.next_action: Tester agent should verify: iconURL(forFeedURL:) round-trips, nil on bad URL, icon(forFeedURL:) returns UIImage on success and nil on failure, concurrent calls deduplicate.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: None — new file with no overlap with existing Common/ files.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: Used Swift actor (not DispatchQueue+class) for natural async/await integration and actor-isolated state. In-flight deduplication uses a [String: Task<UIImage?,Never>] dictionary: a second caller for the same host during a network fetch `await`s the existing Task's value instead of launching a parallel request. The Task body inherits actor isolation (created inside an actor method), so the defer { inFlightTasks.removeValue } executes actor-safely after the value is produced. Alternative considered: AsyncStream or continuation-based fan-out — rejected as unnecessary complexity for this use case. Static iconURL(forFeedURL:) is intentionally non-isolated (no actor hop needed) for cheap, synchronous testability.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: Key paths to verify: (1) iconURL(forFeedURL:) returns correct DuckDuckGo URL for http/https feed URLs and nil for empty string or non-URL; (2) icon(forFeedURL:) returns UIImage when the network mock returns valid PNG data; (3) icon(forFeedURL:) returns nil when URLSession fails or returns non-200; (4) a second call with the same host while the first is in-flight hits the in-flight task path (integration-level concurrent test); (5) disk read path (pre-seed a file in Caches/favicons/ and confirm it is returned without a network call).
handoff_summary.known_limitations: No cache eviction policy beyond NSCache's internal memory pressure handling. Disk files are never expired or pruned — callers relying on fresh favicons would need manual cache clearing. These are acceptable for the initial spec scope.

conflict_risks: None — new file with no overlap with existing Common/ files.

execution_history.summary: Created AggregatorApp/Common/FaviconLoader.swift as a Swift actor with shared singleton. Implements the three-tier lookup (in-memory NSCache → on-disk Caches/favicons/ → network via URLSession.shared), deduplicates concurrent requests for the same host via in-flight Task tracking, and exposes the static iconURL(forFeedURL:) pure helper. Build passes cleanly.

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

## Corrective bead scope

If your `bead_id` ends in `-corrective` or `-corrective-<N>` (e.g. `B-abc12345-corrective`, `B-abc12345-test-corrective-2`), you are running as a CORRECTIVE bead. Your scope is **strictly limited to fixing the specific failure that blocked the parent bead**. Do not:

- Add unrelated improvements, refactors, or cleanup, even if you notice them
- Reapply previously-reverted changes (the operator reverted them for a reason)
- Expand the surface area beyond what is needed to unblock the parent
- Touch files outside what the parent bead's `expected_files` / `expected_globs` covered, unless the failure is genuinely in another file and there is no smaller fix

If you notice an unrelated issue while fixing the corrective scope: **file a separate bead** via `takt bead create` describing what you saw, but do not fix it in this corrective. The operator will prioritize and dispatch.

When in doubt about scope: prefer the smallest possible fix. The reviewer of this corrective will check that the scope was respected.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `Swift` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: ``.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

## Memory

**Read memory at bead start.** Before touching any code, run three searches using `$TAKT_CMD` (injected by the orchestrator):

```bash
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace global
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace feature:<feature_root_id>
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace specs
```

Treat results as ambient context — apply relevant entries; skip entries that don't apply.

**Write to memory when you discover reusable project knowledge** — something that would have changed your approach if you had known it upfront, and is not already in CLAUDE.md or the guardrails.

```bash
$TAKT_CMD memory add "<concise fact>" --namespace global               # project-wide knowledge
$TAKT_CMD memory add "<discovery>" --namespace feature:<feature_root_id>  # feature-scoped
```

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
